coredata.dc:title: Topochemical assembly of levodopa nanoparticles network as a high-performance biosensing platform coupling with π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interactions
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jinhua
Zou, Changpeng
Chen, Can
Fang, Haixiao
Wu, Qiong
Yu, Haidong
Zhu, Jixin
Li, Lin
Yang, Sheng
Huang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Topochemical assembly carbon materials with unique structure rarely investigated and reported. Herein, we demonstrate the feasibility of using topochemical assembly levodopa nanoparticles with dendritic structure (LNDS) network as a new high-performance biosensing platform based on noncovalent functionalization of LNDS with a fluorescent oligonucleotide. The proposed platform is dependent on the competition of π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interactions between LNDS and fluorescent oligonucleotide. The obtained LNDS with 96.1% quenching efficiency is synthesized by using levodopa as the single precursor by natural oxidation or microwave irradiation. The constructed platform can be used for simple and efficient probing single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and cDNA by fluorescence restoration with a highly sensitivity and selectivity, remarkably superior to those based on graphenes. Additionally, an aptasensor is further constructed for small molecule ATP detention in serum with a low detection limit of 4 μM. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to use LNDS to design a biosensing platform, and therefore opens possibilities for new types of nanoparticle-based molecule approaches, and sequencing technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Levodopa nanoparticles
Topochemical assembly
Fluorescent quencher
Aptasensor
ATP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the P1/LNDS for molecular sensing
Interaction mechanism study based on π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion
Application in detection of ATP based on LNDS network
Determination of ATP in human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the ﬁnancial support from the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2017YFA0207201 ), and the National Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 81672508 , 61601218 , 21501091 ), Jiangsu Provincial Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars ( BK20170041 ), and the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (Grant nos. 2018008 ), and the Opening Project of Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing of Shangqiu Normal University ( HKLBRSK1904 ), China- Sweden Joint Mobility Project ( 51811530018 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province ( 2019JM-016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of LNDS: (A–B) The TEM image of LNDS in the Tris-HCl buffer, inset is the photograph and the corresponding size distribution of LNDS; AFM image (C) and depth profiles of as prepared LNDS (D). The UV–visible absorption spectra (E) and FT-IR spectroscopy (F) of LNDS (a) and Levodopa (b).
(A) Schematic of coupling π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interactions between LNDS and ssDNA.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of P1 with different concentrations of LNDS at 480 nm excitation, inset: quenching efficiency of P1 upon the introduction of different concentrations of LNDS; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of P1 under different experimental conditions: (a) P1; (b) P1 + LNDS; (c) P1 + LNDS + cDNA; and (d) P1 + LNDS + mis-DNA.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of P1 and P1/LNDS in the Tris-HCl buffer upon addition of cDNA in the presence of different concentrations of Mg2+ ions; (B) The kinetics of the interaction of P1/LNDS (b) with cDNA (a) in the Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM NaCl, 20 mM Mg2+) at room temperature; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of P1/LNDS at different concentrations of cDNA at 480 nm excitation; (D) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F1/F0) and the cDNA concentration, inset: Linear curve.
Normalized fluorescence intensity of P1, P1+quencher and P1+quencher + cDNA (250 nM) at the same concentration of quenchers (8.56 μg/mL).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of P1/LNDS in the presence of different concentrations of ATP; (B) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F1/F0) and the ATP concentration, inset: Linear curve. (C) The fluorescence intensity changes (F1/F0) of P1/LNDS in the tris-HCl buffer upon addition of ATP in the presence of different concentrations of Mg2+ ions; (D) Selectivity of the ATP-aptamer-based assay for ATP over CTP, GTP, and UTP (150 μM).
Scheme for signaling biomolecular interactions by the assembly of LNDS and dye-labeled ssDNA.
Determination of ATP spiked in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Topochemical assembly of levodopa nanoparticles network as a high-performance biosensing platform coupling with π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interactions

Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University (NanjingTech), 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing, 211816, China

Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Topochemical assembly carbon materials with unique structure rarely investigated and reported. Herein, we demonstrate the feasibility of using topochemical assembly levodopa nanoparticles with dendritic structure (LNDS) network as a new high-performance biosensing platform based on noncovalent functionalization of LNDS with a fluorescent oligonucleotide. The proposed platform is dependent on the competition of π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interactions between LNDS and fluorescent oligonucleotide. The obtained LNDS with 96.1% quenching efficiency is synthesized by using levodopa as the single precursor by natural oxidation or microwave irradiation. The constructed platform can be used for simple and efficient probing single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and cDNA by fluorescence restoration with a highly sensitivity and selectivity, remarkably superior to those based on graphenes. Additionally, an aptasensor is further constructed for small molecule ATP detention in serum with a low detection limit of 4 μM. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to use LNDS to design a biosensing platform, and therefore opens possibilities for new types of nanoparticle-based molecule approaches, and sequencing technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the attractive thermal, electronic and mechanical properties, carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes and graphenes, have been promising candidates for electronic, chemical, and biological applications [1–4]. Specifically, it has been demonstrated these nanostructures have the ability to act as “nano-scaffold” for oligonucleotides and “nano-quencher” for dyes [5,6]. Therefore, these systems have emerged as the extensively studied materials for sensor applications in fluorescence detection of nucleic acids [7], proteins [8], small molecules [9,10], and metal ions [11–13], etc. While these nanomaterials have shown distinct advantages in biosensor design, the hydrophobic and huge specific surface areas of graphite will produce strong π-π stacking interaction with the adsorbed oligonucleotides [14], which forms the high energy barrier that eliminate the possibility of gaining receivable endpoints. Therefore, their applications in rapid, sensitive, and real-time bioanalysis are limited, and searching for benign alternatives of similar optical, electronic and mechanical characteristics is expected.

Recently, carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials have been discovered to be chemically inert, biologically compatible and low cytotoxicity [15–17]. Studies on the synthesis and optical features of CNPs have been undertaken [16,18–21], but the biological applications of the nanostructures remain to be explored. Li et al. and Liu et al. discovered that, after surface passivation, quantum-sized carbon dots are photoluminescent and can be used for luminescent images in vitro [22] or in vivo [23]. However, the CNPs prepared by common routes have a broad size distribution with uncertain emission regions and low quantum yields, which limit the efficiency for biosensing and image. Aside from the intrinsic luminescence of CNPs, noncovalent assembly of CNPs with a fluorescent biomolecule can not only be scalable, but also provide the materials with processibility and new functions. At this time, there are plenty of such publications in the fields of carbon nanotubes [24–27] and graphenes [28–31]. In the present work, therefore, we try to our attempt for functionalization of levodopa nanoparticles with dendritic structure (LNDS) with fluorescent oligonucleotides and explore it as a new biosensing platform.

Herein, coupling fluorescent biomolecules and carbon materials to develop high performance biosensors is a promising application of carbon materials in nanotechnology. As the continuation of our studies on fluorescent nucleic acid probe based on carbon nanotubes and CNPs [32–34], we demonstrate here the feasibility of using topochemical assembly LNDS as a new high-performance biosensing platform by noncovalent functionalization of LNDS with a fluorescent oligonucleotide. The developed platform is based on the interaction mechanism of π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion. The adsorption of fluorescent oligonucleotides on LNDS is due to competition interaction of the hydrophobic and electrostatic repulsion between the nanoparticles and oligonucleotides. Thus, the difference in electrostatic properties of ss-DNA and ds-DNA will result in different adsorb behavior on the nanoparticle surfaces [35,36]. In particular, fluorescence from a dye-tagged ssDNA probe is quenched by LNDS, unless the probe binds to a target molecule (Scheme 1 ). We show that this approach, which is remarkably superior to those based on common carbon nanomaterials, can be used for high-performance biosensing by fluorescence restoration.

Tri(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis.MO, USA). Levodopa (3-hydroxy-l-tyrosin, C9H11NO4) was purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the DNA samples, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), cytidine triphosphate (CTP), guanosine triphosphate (GTP), and uridine triphosphate (UTP) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fresh human blood samples were collected randomly from the healthy volunteers at 2nd Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing Medical University. The blood was subjected to centrifugation at 2000 rmp for 5 min and supernatant liquid were collected as Human Serum Albumin (HSA). All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1750, SHIMADZU，Japan) was applied to record the UV–Vis absorption spectra. An F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) was adopted to record the fluorescence spectra with a standard 10 × 10 mm quartz cell and a xenon lamp as the excitation source. A microwave synthesizer (Initiator +, Biotage, Sweden) was used to synthesize MLN (levodopa nanoparticles synthesized by microwave irradiation). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was acquired utilizing a Milli-Q Advantage A10 purification system (Millipore S.A, Bedford, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2010 UHR transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

0.03 g of levodopa was added to 15 mL ultrapure water at room temperature without complex preparation requirements, the color of the solution changed from colorless to black after nine month in the dark rooms, indicating the formation of levodopa nanoparticles. Then 13.4 mg black flake solid (LNDS 0.01 M 10 mL) was obtained by freeze-drying the obtained solution for 36 h in a vacuum freeze-dryer.

2 mg levodopa was mixed with 10.0 mL H2O, and stirred for 1 h at 100 °C under microwave irradiation. And the conditions of microwave irradiation were as follows: microwave power was 100 W, the voltage was 220 V, the frequency was 50 Hz. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixed black solution was freeze-dried for 24 h in vacuum freeze dryer. Then 4.1 mg MLN (levodopa nanoparticles synthesized by microwave irradiation) were obtained after freeze drying.

After the synthesis of LNDS, enough LNDS was introduced into the Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM pH = 7.4) containing 37.5 nM P1, and the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 15 min to form the P1/LNDS nanocomplex. Then cDNA or ATP with different concentrations varying from 0 to 200 μM were added. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded from 505 to 650 nm at an excitation wavelength of 480 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm was used for quantitative analysis. To measure specificity, 150 μM ATP, UTP, GTP or CTP was added.

ATP was spiked into the diluted human serum solution with a series of concentrations (8, 12, and 16 μM) and the spiked human serum samples were measured with the proposed method, which was repeated for three times. The test results were compared with each other and then the average recovery was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the P1/LNDS for molecular sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of fluorescent oligonucleotides on LNDS is due to competition interaction of the hydrophobic and electrostatic repulsion between the nanoparticles and oligonucleotides. Thus, the difference in electrostatic properties of ss-DNA and ds-DNA will result in different adsorb behavior on the nanoparticle surfaces [35,36]. In particular, fluorescence from a dye-tagged ssDNA probe is quenched by LNDS, unless the probe binds to a target molecule (Scheme 1). We show that this approach, which is remarkably superior to those based on common carbon nanomaterials, can be used for high-performance biosensing by fluorescence restoration.

To characterize the morphology of LNDS, TEM images indicate that LNDS had dendritic structure composed of nanoparticles with size distribution from 10 to 40 nm, and the majority of sizes is 27.5 nm (Fig. 1 A and B). Different batches of LNDS have similar dendritic structure network (Fig. S1). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement is estimated to confirm the size distribution of LNDS (Fig. S2), which is larger than that we observed by TEM, due to containing the overall hydrodynamic diameter. Fig. 1C and D shows the AFM and typical section analysis, which indicate that LNDS had dendritic structure network with thickness distribution from 10 to 30 nm. Fig. 1E shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of the levodopa (black line) and LNDS (red line). LNDS has only an absorption peak at 415 nm to that with levodopa, and the corresponding photographs of the LNDS with different concentrations (0.5–0.01 M) under room-light are shown in Fig. S3.

The chemical groups of LNDS surface show N–H stretching mode at 3440 cm−1, C–H stretching mode at 3158 cm−1. The peak at 1625 cm−1 is the bending vibration of N–H or stretching vibration of CN. The band at 1393 cm−1 corresponding to the bending vibration of C–N (Fig. 1F). LNDS is composed of C, N, and O elements (64.92%, 2.96%, and 32.12%) (Fig. S4A). The XPS spectrum of C 1s suggests the existence of CO (286.4 eV) and CC (284.8 eV) bonds in the LNDS (Fig. S4B). While the N 1s spectrum includes three parts corresponding to N–C/N–H (400.2 eV), NH4 + (402.0 eV), and NC (400.5 eV), respectively (Fig. S4C). The O 1s spectrum is divided into three bands at 532.2, 532.7 and 533.6 eV, contributing to O–H, OC and O–C bonds, respectively (Fig. S4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism study based on π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion
ce:section.ce:para: Recent work has reported that via π-π stacking interactions ssDNA is able to adsorb on the surface of carbon nanomaterial surfaces [37]. However, for LNDS there is a different adsorption behavior due to the hydrophobic surface with hydroxyl group (Fig. 2 ). For one thing, ssDNA including hydrophobic aromatic structures could adsorb on the hydrophobic regions of the LNDS surfaces via the π-π stacking. For another, DNA carry phosphoric acid skeleton with negative charge, and the electrostatic repulsion should reduce the adsorption of DNA on the LNDS surfaces. The competitive interactions would produce a completely different adsorption behavior for DNA and LNDS. When the electrostatic repulsion is less than the π-π stacking interaction, the DNA will be adsorbed on the surfaces of LNDS, resulting in the quenching of dye on labeled DNA.

To confirm electrostatic repulsion between LNDS and ssDNA, we chose a fluorescein (FAM)-tagged 23-base oligonucleotide (P1, Table S1) to investigate how ionic strength affect the interaction of LNDS and P1 via P1 fluorescence quenching by LNDS. The fluorescence spectrum of P1 shows significant fluorescence increase because of the presence of the dye. But significant fluorescence decrease was observed in the presence of LNDS, suggesting that the ssDNA was adsorbed on the surface of LNDS, promoting the occurrence of energy and electron transfer processes [30,37]. With the concentration of LNDS varying from 0 to 28 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensities of FAM-DNA decreased gradually (Fig. 3 A and B). The relative linear curve of quenching efficiency at different LNDS concentration is provided in Fig. S5 and 18.8 μg/mL LNDS was deemed to be optimal for the building of the P1/LNDS when the quenching efficiency reached 96.1%. Meanwhile, the effect of Mg2+ on the adsorption of P1 on LNDS was investigated in buffer solution, completely different quenching capabilities for LNDS in the absence and the presence of Mg2+ could be observed. As shown in Fig. S6, in the absence of Mg2+ ions, the P1 fluorescence intensity was reduced to around 23.34% by 12.5 μg/mL LNDS. However, in the presence of 10.0 mM Mg2+ and the same amount of LNDS in the solution, the emission of P1 with LNDS had 88.78% fluorescence quenching. With the increase of the Mg2+ concentration, the P1 fluorescence intensity decreased. In the presence of 20 mM Mg2+ reached, the assembly of P1/LNDS achieved about ~96.1% fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4 A), as further increasing the concentration of Mg2+, no great changes could be found. While with the increase of NaCl concentration in solution under the same condition, the emission of P1 was gradually increased (Figs. S7C and D). These results suggested that metal ions can facilitate DNA adsorption onto LNDS. It is preliminarily indicated that the interaction between P1 and LNDS can be regulated by adjusting electrostatic repulsion at low salt concentration. The phenomena observed here supported the concept that Mg2+ promoted adsorption of ssDNA to LNDS. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of sensor increased as increasing the concentration of Na+ (from 0 to 600 mM), indicating that high ion concentration could destroy the adsorption of ssDNA to LNDS and resulted in the recovery of P1 fluorescence. The result proved that there was electrostatic repulsion effect between P1 and LNDS. Meantime, the recovery efficiency of P1 fluorescence was only 35% in the presence of high concentrations of Na+, there were other interaction mechanism. Considering that the properties of LNDS are similar to those of some carbon materials reported previously, which is proved by TEM, AFM, FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS spectrum. These results preliminarily demonstrate that interaction between P1 and LNDS is a result of competition between π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion forces. In addition, we checked the selectivity of metal ions and carried out quantitative analysis in the presence of metal ions. The sensor has good selectivity for Mg2+ in the presecne of other interfering ions (Fig. S8). To find out quantitative analysis in the presence of metal ions, some metal ions (Ba2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, K+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Na+) were added into the system. The sensor also exhibited a good linear correlation (F1/F0 = 0.01229CcDNA + 1.02822, R2 = 0.9589) to the cDNA concentrations ranging from 5 to 50 nM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.7 nM (Fig. S9), where F1 and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm with and without cDNA, respectively.

The DNA/LNDS complex could be dissociated by Watson-Crick base-pairing with a complementary sequence. Fig. 4C shows the effect of the cDNA of P1 (T1, Table S1) on the fluorescence of P1/LNDS. 12-fold fluorescence enhancement was observed in the presence of one equiv T1, suggesting that adsorption of dsDNA on LNDS surface is significantly weaker than ssDNA. This essential difference is that ssDNA can be fully expanded to reveal its bases for π-π stacking interaction with the hydrophobic region of LNDS surface. Yet dsDNA has the stable double-helix geometry, which always faces the phosphate backbone with negatively charge to shield the nucleobases from interacting with the LNDS surface [33]. Under these conditions, electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate and hydroxyl groups in LNDS leads the interaction between the carbon and dsDNA. The different affinity of LNDS for ssDNA over dsDNA could be used to detect DNA hybridization. The time scan shows that incubation time of this hybridization reaction was determined to be 15 min (Fig. 4B). The sensor exhibited a good linear correlation (F1/F0 = 0.16352CcDNA + 1.1284, R2 = 0.99409) to the cDNA concentrations ranging from 0 to 45 nM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1 nM (Fig. 4C and D), where F1 and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm with and without cDNA, respectively. Compared to the platform based on graphenes, our constructed platform exhibits higher quenching efficiency and better recovery efficiency (Fig. S10 and Fig. 5 ), which is attributed to the special network structure and the mechanism of π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interaction. We also tested the reversibility of the sensor (Fig. S11), and the increase of the number of cycles (3 times) had little effect on the response of the cDNA. Moreover, the sensor platform consisting of MLN also responds to the cDNA (Fig. S12), when the concentration of MLN is 16.4 μg/mL and cDNA concentration is 250 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in detection of ATP based on LNDS network
ce:section.ce:para: ATP is composed of adenine, ribose and three phosphoric groups, it can releases more energy when hydrolyzed and is the most direct energy sources in organisms. Referring to previous studies and the good water-solubility of the PDA-like nanoparticles [38–40], we put forward that the assembly of P1/LNDS may be suitable for ATP detection depending on the fluorescent “turn on” strategy. When P1/LNDS was mixed with ATP, the fluorescence intensity gradually recovered as increasing ATP concentrations, when ATP concentration reached 200 μM, the fluorescence intensity tended to be stable (Fig. 6 A). The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of FAM-DNA and the concentrations of ATP ranged from 4 to 25 μM (F1/F0 = 0.08326 CATP+1.03783 R2 = 0.98789), and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.2 μM (Fig. 6B) compared with some previously reported methods about ATP (Table S2) [41–46]. The detection limit was defined by the equation LOD=(3σ/S), where σ is the standard error of the intercept and S was the slope of the calibration curve. The effect of Mg2+ concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of systems was also investigated, and 20 mM Mg2+ was used for the next fluorescence tests (Fig. 6C).

In addition, the selectivity of the P1/LNDS for ATP detection was investigated. Three analogues CTP, GTP, and UTP were incubated with P1/LNDS under the same conditions, only ATP induced an obvious increase of F1/F0 (Fig. 6D). These results demonstrated that the assembly can serve as a sensitive ATP-specific fluorescent assay, and it was hopeful that will be used in the selective and efficient detection of ATP in biological environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ATP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed sensor was further applied for ATP determination in diluted human serum samples. The different concentrations of standard ATP were reacted with P1/LNDS in 1% human serum samples for the detection. A recovery experiment was performed on the standard ATP solution (8, 12, and 16 μM) spiked with ATP. The obtained recoveries of the samples were listed in Table 1 . It was shown that the recoveries was between 95% and 110%, suggesting that the novel approach can be used for the detection of ATP in complex samples with high selectivity.

In conclusion, we have proved that LNDS possess high efficient fluorescence quenching ability and different affinities for ss- and dsDNA. Depending on these results, a new approach using fluorescent oligonucleotide-conjugated LNDS for recognizing and detecting biomolecules was proposed. The prominent advantages of this sensor include the following: The design of the present method is facile that need not any lengthy protocols and sophisticated probe synthesis, but can be designed in a way that offer unique advantages and capabilities that traditional molecular systems cannot provide. (ii) The as-prepared LNDS have high quenching efficiency to dye-labeled DNA up to 96.1%. (iii) This is the first attempt to use LNDS to design a biosensing platform by coupling π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion interactions, and therefore opens possibilities for new types of nanoparticle-based molecule approaches, and sequencing technologies. Although the current study is our first attempt to connect this LNDS with DNA and protein sensing, on the basis of the biologically compatible, low cytotoxicity, and cellular delivery ability of LNDS, this approach will be promising in finding challenging applications involving cellular and physiological problems.

Jinhua Liu,designed the experimentwrote the manuscript, Changpeng Zou,performed the experimen thandled the data, Can Chen, performed the experimen thandled the, Haixiao Fang, designed the experiment performed the experiment, Qiong Wu,handled the data, Haidong Yu,handled the data, Jixin Zhu,designed the experiment revised the manuscript, Lin Li,revised the manuscript, Sheng Yang, performed the experiment; W. Huang revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the ﬁnancial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFA0207201), and the National Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 81672508, 61601218, 21501091), Jiangsu Provincial Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (BK20170041), and the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (Grant nos. 2018008), and the Opening Project of Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing of Shangqiu Normal University (HKLBRSK1904), China-Sweden Joint Mobility Project (51811530018), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2019JM-016).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121285.
